title: The Bystander Effect
description: Why do people fail to help when others are present? Watch diffusion of responsibility emerge from group size.

crowd_size.label: Crowd Size
crowd_size.description: How many people witness the event

narration.text: Someone needs help (red). The crowd witnesses the situation but hasn't yet acted.
In a crowd, each person assumes someone else will help. 'There are 20 of us — surely someone more qualified will step in.'
This is diffusion of responsibility. The more witnesses, the less any individual feels personally obligated to act.
Research by Darley & Latané (1968): alone, people helped in 70% of emergencies. In groups of 6, only 31% helped.
To break the bystander effect: make direct eye contact with one specific person and say their name or describe them — 'You, in the blue jacket, call an ambulance.'
The bystander effect operates in online abuse, workplace harassment, and financial fraud. Diffused responsibility enables inaction at every scale.
